one_line_takeaway: Deliberately weakening each layer of a distortion-free LLM watermark ensemble (via a λ-interpolation with the base distribution) preserves token entropy across layers and thereby improves both detection accuracy and robustness to paraphrasing attacks — more haste, less speed.
key_finding: Weakening the per-layer watermark consistently IMPROVES both detectability and attack robustness, contradicting the stronger-is-better convention. On C4 with Llama-3.2-3B at 150 tokens (TPR@FPR=0.1%): SynthID improves from 75.1% (λ=1) to 79.7% (λ=0.8), and ENS-MCMark from 77.2% to 78.5%; median p-values also drop (e.g., SynthID 1.33e-7 → 1.57e-8). Gains are larger on Mistral-7B (SynthID 73.1% → 78.8% at 150 tokens). Peak detectability is reached at moderate weakening (about λ=0.8 and α=0.4), not the weakest setting. Robustness (Table 2, C4, Llama, 250 tokens, TPR@FPR=0.1%): the weaker configuration wins under every attack — e.g. random token replacement SynthID 81.1%→84.8% and ENS-MCMark 85.9%→87.5%; GPT back-translation SynthID 51.8%→54.2%; DIPPER SynthID 8.7%→10.3%. Empirically (Fig. 3) entropy and expected green ratio are strongly positively correlated, and (Figs. 5/6) weaker settings preserve higher per-layer entropy and green ratio across layers, confirming the entropy-decay theory. DIPPER/GPT-rephrase attacks remain hard for all configs (TPR in the single digits to mid-teens), so the improvement is relative, not a solved problem.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a counter-intuitive "weaker-is-better" framework for distortion-free LLM watermark ensembles. Whereas prevailing ensemble methods maximize each single layer's watermark strength, the authors show this is self-defeating and instead propose deliberately WEAKENING each single-layer watermark. Concretely they define an interpolated watermarking function Fλ(P,k) = λ·F(P,k) + (1−λ)·P (Eq. 14), where F is any existing distortion-free watermark and λ∈[0,1] tunes strength (λ=1 recovers the original watermark, smaller λ is weaker). They prove (Theorems 4.3, 4.4) that this interpolation stays distortion-free and preserves more entropy per layer. The supporting theoretical contribution is an entropy-based account of ensemble detectability: (i) detectability is bounded by token-distribution entropy (closed-form for SynthID: expected green ratio g = 3/4 − 1/4·Σ P(x)² ≤ 3/4 − 1/4·exp(−H(P))); and (ii) Theorems 4.1/4.2 show distortion-free watermarking monotonically decreases both entropy and expected green ratio across ensemble layers (via Jensen's inequality on the concave entropy/green-ratio functionals), so stacking strong layers accelerates signal collapse. Preserving entropy with a weaker per-layer watermark lets the ensemble accumulate statistical evidence more efficiently over longer text. NOTE: SynthID, DiPmark, MCMark, BiMark, and the Wu et al. (2025a) ensemble framework are cited prior work used as baselines/base watermarks — NOT this paper's inventions; the paper's novelty is the λ-interpolation weakening scheme and the entropy-decay analysis.
threat_model: Content-provenance / attribution setting for AI-generated text. Defender embeds a distortion-free watermark ensemble in LLM output to later detect and attribute machine-generated content (misinformation, copyright misuse). Adversary seeks to evade detection by perturbing watermarked text to remove the signal; the paper evaluates four concrete removal attacks — random token replacement, GPT back-translation, GPT rephrasing, and DIPPER paraphrasing — measuring residual true-positive detection rate at a fixed 0.1% false-positive rate. The claimed defensive advance is that a weaker per-layer watermark strengthens post-attack detectability while remaining provably distortion-free (output quality preserved in expectation).
limitations: Authors state two limitations: (1) evaluation only on open-source models of 3B–7B parameters (Llama-3.2-3B, Mistral-7B) — no large or closed models; (2) no adaptive strategy that dynamically tunes watermark strength from real-time token entropy, which could further optimize the trade-off. Additional reviewer-noted gaps: the strong-attack regime (GPT rephrase, DIPPER) still yields very low TPR (single digits to ~16%) for all configurations, so robustness gains are marginal there; the entropy-bounds detectability result is closed-form only for SynthID, with DiPmark/MCMark supported by empirical correlation plus concavity proofs rather than a closed-form entropy bound; and comparisons are strength-ablations of the authors' own weakening applied to three base watermarks rather than against a distinct competing ensemble design.

datasets_used: Longform QA from WaterBench (Tu et al., 2023)
quantitative_results: C4, Llama-3.2-3B, 150 tokens, TPR@FPR=0.1%: SynthID 75.1% (λ=1) → 79.7% (λ=0.8); median p-value 1.33e-7 → 1.57e-8
C4, Mistral-7B, 150 tokens, TPR@FPR=0.1%: SynthID 73.1% (λ=1) → 78.8% (λ=0.8); ENS-MCMark 78.5% → 82.2%
C4, Llama-3.2-3B, 250 tokens, TPR@FPR=0.1%: ENS-MCMark 95.1% (λ=1) → 96.7% (λ=0.8), median p-value 8.75e-15 → 3.91e-15
Robustness (C4, Llama, 250 tokens, TPR@FPR=0.1%): random token replacement SynthID 81.1%→84.8%, ENS-MCMark 85.9%→87.5%; GPT back-translation SynthID 51.8%→54.2%, ENS-MCMark 55.3%→58.9%
Robustness under strong paraphrase: GPT rephrase SynthID 12.7%→13.0%, ENS-MCMark 13.8%→16.4%; DIPPER SynthID 8.7%→10.3%, ENS-MCMark 12.3%→12.7% (all remain low)
Ablation: peak TPR around λ=0.8 and α=0.4; over-weakening degrades performance
Theory: SynthID expected green ratio g = 3/4 − 1/4·Σ_x P(x)² ≤ 3/4 − 1/4·exp(−H(P)); Theorems 4.1/4.2 prove monotonic entropy and green-ratio decrease across ensemble layers
Config: SynthID m=30 layers; ENS-DiPmark n=5; ENS-MCMark n=5, l=20; run on 4× NVIDIA RTX 6000 Ada GPUs
baselines_compared: SynthID (Dathathri et al., 2024) at strong λ=1 vs weakened λ=0.8
ENS-DiPmark (Wu et al., 2023, 2025a) at strong α=0.5 vs weakened α=0.4
ENS-MCMark (Chen et al., 2025; Wu et al., 2025a) at strong λ=1 vs weakened λ=0.8
